coredata.dc:title: Numerical simulations and electrochemical experiments of the mass transfer of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ming
Du, Liqun
Qi, Leijie
Li, Yuanqi
Li, Yu
Li, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: This paper explores the mass transfer mechanism of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation by numerical simulations and electrochemical experiments. Firstly, the velocity distribution of electroforming solution inside the microvias under ultrasound treatment is simulated by COMSOL Multiphysics software. The ultrasonic frequency is that of 120 kHz. The ultrasonic powers are 100 W, 200 W, 300 W and 400 W, respectively. The simulation results indicate that the mean liquid velocity inside the microvias increases with the increasing of acoustic power. In addition, under a certain ultrasonic power, the mean liquid velocity will decrease with increasing the distance between microvias and transducer, the aspect ratio of microvias and the distance between cathode and central position. Secondly, electrochemical experiments are presented to investigate the effect of ultrasonic agitation on the electrode kinetics of microvias electroforming. It is found that ultrasonic treatment decreases the thickness of diffusion layer, increases the limiting diffusion current densities and further enhances the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. Compared with the silent condition, the diffusion layer thicknesses with the acoustic power of 100 W, 200 W, 300 W, 400 W are decreased by 50.0%, 64.1%, 69.3% and 74.5%, respectively. Finally, according to the results above, the 200 × 200 metal micro-column array structures are fabricated by ultrasonic electroforming under the condition of 120 kHz and 200 W. The metal micro-column is 250 μm high and has a diameter of 80 μm. The results show that ultrasonic electroforming can enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming, and further solve the problem of porous structure in electroforming layer. This work contributes to expanding the application of ultrasonic agitation in the microvias electroforming.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microvias electroforming
Ultrasonic
Simulation
Electrochemical experiments
Micro-column array structures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation modeling (coupling of acoustic pressure field and flow field)
Effect of distance between the microvias and transducer
Effect of the distance between cathode and central position
Fabrication of the metal micro-column array structures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of geometric structure.
Simulation results of liquid velocity distributions inside microvias under different ultrasonic powers (a): 100 W, (b): 200 W, (c): 300 W, (d): 400 W.
Mean velocities inside microvias with different ultrasonic powers.
Simulation result of pressure field distributions inside microvias under different ultrasonic powers (a): 100 W, (b): 200 W, (c): 300 W, (d): 400 W.
Mean velocities inside microvias with different distances between the microvias and transducer.
Simulation results of liquid velocity distributions in microvias under different aspect ratios. (a) 0.5, (b) 1.5, (c) 4.
Simulation results of pressure field distributions in microvias under different aspect ratios. (a) 0.5, (b) 1.5, (c) 4.
Mean velocities inside microvias with the different distances between cathode and central position.
Schematic diagram of the working electrode.
Schematic diagram of the experimental set-up.
Cathodic polarization curves without ultrasonic and under different ultrasonic powers.
SEM image of the metal micro-column array structures. (a) Without ultrasonic wave; (b) With ultrasonic wave.
The densities and sound velocities of mediums.
The calculation results of diffusion layer thickness.

head.ce:title: Numerical simulations and electrochemical experiments of the mass transfer of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Technology and System of Liaoning Province, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Technology and System of Liaoning Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Precision and Non-traditional Machining Technology of Ministry of Education, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Precision and Non-traditional Machining Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Technology and System of Liaoning Province, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Technology and System of Liaoning Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper explores the mass transfer mechanism of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation by numerical simulations and electrochemical experiments. Firstly, the velocity distribution of electroforming solution inside the microvias under ultrasound treatment is simulated by COMSOL Multiphysics software. The ultrasonic frequency is that of 120 kHz. The ultrasonic powers are 100 W, 200 W, 300 W and 400 W, respectively. The simulation results indicate that the mean liquid velocity inside the microvias increases with the increasing of acoustic power. In addition, under a certain ultrasonic power, the mean liquid velocity will decrease with increasing the distance between microvias and transducer, the aspect ratio of microvias and the distance between cathode and central position. Secondly, electrochemical experiments are presented to investigate the effect of ultrasonic agitation on the electrode kinetics of microvias electroforming. It is found that ultrasonic treatment decreases the thickness of diffusion layer, increases the limiting diffusion current densities and further enhances the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. Compared with the silent condition, the diffusion layer thicknesses with the acoustic power of 100 W, 200 W, 300 W, 400 W are decreased by 50.0%, 64.1%, 69.3% and 74.5%, respectively. Finally, according to the results above, the 200 × 200 metal micro-column array structures are fabricated by ultrasonic electroforming under the condition of 120 kHz and 200 W. The metal micro-column is 250 μm high and has a diameter of 80 μm. The results show that ultrasonic electroforming can enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming, and further solve the problem of porous structure in electroforming layer. This work contributes to expanding the application of ultrasonic agitation in the microvias electroforming.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of micro-electro-mechanical system (MEMS) technology, microvias electroforming has good potential applications to fabricate microarray electrodes [1,2], biopotential electrodes [3–5] metal molds [6], etc. However, there is a technical issue in electroforming microvias with a high aspect ratio and small diameter. Influenced by the small diameter and high aspect ratio, microvias electroforming faces the problem of poor mass transfer, which easily causes the problem of porous structure in electroforming layer, finally leads to the fabrication failure [7,8] . These problems limit the development of microvias electroforming to a certain extent. Hence, it is significant to investigate the methods of improving the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. Some efforts have been made to improve the mass transfer of microvias electroforming, such as using various additive chemistries [9,10], adding a moving cathode [11], using an air-pressure agitation, adding a thermal gradient [12], adopting pulse-reversed current [13], and optimizing process parameters [14]. But the improvement effect of these methods is unsatisfactory.

In recent years, experimental studies and numerical simulations have shown that ultrasonic agitation is helpful to improve mass transfer. Wang [15] studied the Through Silicon Vias (TVS) filling quality with ultrasonic frequency of 20 kHz and ultrasonic power of 60 W. Microvias with an aspect ratio of 3 (20 μm in diameter and 60 μm in depth) were used in experiments. The results show that the TSV filling quality can be improved by the ultrasonic electroplating. Chen [16] compared the effect of ultrasonic agitation and mechanical agitation on the microvias electroforming. The microvias used in the experiments had a diameter of 4 μm and depth of 12 μm. The ultrasonic power and frequency used for electroplating agitation were 400 W and 27 kHz, respectively. The experimental results demonstrate that ultrasonic agitation is superior to mechanical agitation in enhancing the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. Jones [17] applied high frequency ultrasonic to the electroforming of microvias with an aspect ratio of 5.7:1 (175 μm in diameter). Although the microvias still maintain void within the center, the deposit thickness is measured to increase by 320% on the application of high frequency ultrasonic. It is considered that high frequency acoustics increases the transport of electroforming solution and further ensure efficient plating reactions that support high quality depositions. The above works qualitatively analyze the phenomenon that ultrasound can enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. However, these studies only discuss the effect of single ultrasonic power on microvias electroforming with a certain aspect ratio. The effect of parameters, such as ultrasonic power and dimension of microvias on the mass transfer of microvias electroforming is seldom investigated. Costello [18] used micro-particle imaging velocimetry (micro-PIV) to analyze the effect of high-frequency ultrasonic on microvias electroforming with different aspect ratios. The experiment results show that acoustic streaming plays an important role in removing bubbles and promoting ion transportation. Nevertheless, the microvias used in the micro-PIV should be fabricated by transparent material, which limits the application of micro-PIV. Compton [19] and Atobe [20] investigated the mass transport of electrochemical reactions under the effect of ultrasonic treatment. The studies show that ultrasonic treatment decreases the thickness of diffusion layer, and increases the limiting current controlled by mass transport. Nevertheless, few electrochemical experiments have been reported on the microvias electroforming and the electrode kinetics of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation.

On the other hand, numerical simulation study has been used to better understand the effect of ultrasonic on the mass transfer. Nilson and Griffiths [21] used analytical and numerical methods to solve the equations governing flow and species transport by acoustic streaming within trench-like cavities. The results show that acoustic streaming provides the necessary drag force for mass transfer of solution. The analytical and numerical study is performed based on development, the reflected wave of polymer surface at the mold bottom is weak, so the reflected wave is not included in any calculations. But for the microvias electroforming, the electroforming surface at the microvias bottom has an acoustic impedance much greater than that of the electrolyte. In this case, the reflected wave strongly influences the velocity field inside the microvias. Xu Zheng [22] used numerical simulation to investigate the liquid flow distribution in a sonochemical reactor at high frequency. And the effect of sonochemical reactor parameters, such as acoustic power and dimensions of reactor and transducer, on the liquid velocity distribution in reactor was investigated. The simulations included the effect of reflected wave and the results provided optimization on the design of sonochemical reactor. However, the simulations only studied the effect of ultrasonic treatment on the mass transfer in macro-scale, and the calculations have not been extended to small scales.

In this paper, numerical simulations and electrochemical experiments are performed to study the mass transfer of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation. Firstly, based on the above research, the liquid velocity distribution of ultrasonic induced flow inside the microvias is numerically simulated. The factors that affect the liquid velocity distribution inside the microvias are analyzed, such as ultrasonic power, distance between the microvias and transducer, aspect ratio of microvias, distance between cathode and central position. These simulation results can provide the optimum experiment parameters for microvias electroforming. Secondly, electrochemical experiments are presented to investigate the effect of ultrasonic agitation on the electrode kinetics of microvias electroforming. Meanwhile, the simulation is validated by the experimental results. Finally, a metal micro-column array structures is electroformed by ultrasonic agitation to prove the feasibility of this research achievements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation modeling (coupling of acoustic pressure field and flow field)

ce:section.ce:para:
The time harmonic wave equation (inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation) in media can be described as [23]:
(1)
∇ ·
-
1
ρ c
∇ p
-
ω 2
ρ c
c
c
2
p
= 0
Here, p is acoustic pressure, ω gives the angular frequency of ultrasound,
ρ c
=
Z c
k c
/ ω
is complex density and
c c
= ω /
k
c
represents the complex sound speed. And the complex wave number k c and the impedance Z c are expressed as follows:
(2)
k c
=
ω
c s
- i α ,
Z c
= ρ
c s
where
α
is the absorption coefficient,
c s
and ρ denote sound speed in the media and density of the media respectively.

The schematic diagram of geometric structure is shown in Fig. 1 . The model has been established on the basis of microvias electroforming under ultrasound agitation. The microvias used in electroforming are fabricated by UV-LIGA technology. The calculations have been performed in a quartz tank with 90 mm in height, 80 mm in width and 4 mm in thickness. The quartz tank is filled with electroforming solution. The cathode consists of the nickel substrate, the photoresist, and the microvias. The microvias are distributed in 5 positions. Each position has 3 microvias with 80 μm in diameter and 120 μm in height. The ambient temperature is 293 K. A transducer operated at 120 kHz is mounted at the bottom of quartz tank center with a radius of 10 mm. In consideration of the effect of cavitation bubbles, the absorption coefficient 1 m−1 [22,24] is selected in simulation. As the electroforming equipment is mirror symmetry, the right half of the quartz tank in two dimensions is calculated. Thus, transducer center along the propagation direction is set as axial symmetry.

The impedance boundary was utilized to specify the boundary condition of electroforming solution-air, electroforming solution-quartz glass, electroforming solution-photoresist, electroforming solution-nickel substrate and quartz glass-air interfaces. The impedance boundary is written as [25]:
(3)
n →
·
-
1
ρ c
∇ p
=
i ω p
Z e
where
Z e
=
ρ e
c e
is the acoustic input impedance of the external domain (
ρ e
and
c e
is the density and sound speed of the external domain) and
n →
is unit normal vector against wall. The densities and sound velocities of mediums are shown in Table 1 .

For the transducer, the boundary was set as acoustic pressure. The input powers of transducer used in the simulation were 100 W, 200 W, 300 W, 400 W, respectively. Initial value of acoustic pressure in water, quartz glass, nickel substrate and electroforming solution was set as 0. The calculations were carried out by COMSOL Multiphysics 4.3. The calculation area consists of about 178,762 elements and in calculation the degree of freedom is about 371,674.

The flow field was calculated by two equations, the momentum transport equation [26]:
(4)
- ∇ ·
- p
I →
+ μ
∇ u +
∇ u
T
= F
and the continuity equation:
(5)
∇ · u
= 0
where
I →
is the unit matrix, u, μ and p are the liquid velocity, liquid viscosity and pressure in liquid respectively. F represents the force per unit volume that causes streaming. Superscript T denotes the transpose matrix. In this paper, we presume constant density of the liquid throughout the electroforming tank.

During the calculation of flow field, the volume force is expressed as [27]:
(6)
F
=
2 α
ρ 0
c
0
2
|p|
2

The no slip wall was used as the boundary condition to specify a stationary solid wall and transducer surface. This boundary condition is prescribed as:
(7)
u
= 0

At the solution surface, the slip wall has been used to specify no viscous effects at the liquid surface. This boundary condition is prescribed as:
(8)
u ·
n →
= 0

The viscosity of electroforming solution is 0.7 × 10−3 Pa s. The initial liquid velocity in electroforming solution was set as 0 and the thermal convection effect has not been taken into account. In calculation, the acoustic pressure distributions by numerical simulation were used to obtain the volume force in Eq. (6) and the liquid velocity distribution was calculated using the momentum transport Eq. (4).

Fig. 2 shows the simulation results of liquid velocity distribution inside the microvias under different ultrasonic powers. The given microvias are located in the middle of the substrate. The white flow streamlines and color in figure represent the velocity direction and magnitude, respectively.

When the microvias electroforming is carried out without ultrasound, the liquid velocity is assumed to be 0. Because under the silent condition, there is no volume force to act on the electroforming solution, the solution is almost in static state. In this case, the mass transfer of ions is controlled by diffusion and the mass transfer efficiency is very low. This easily causes a decrease in ion concentration on the cathode surface and further forms porous structure in the microvias. However, when the microvias electroforming is carried out with ultrasound, the liquid velocity inside microvias significantly increased. As seen in Fig. 2(a)–(d), under the effect of ultrasound, the fluid flow is induced and forms a single vortex inside the microvias. And the liquid velocity inside microvias increases with the increase in ultrasonic power. Under the effect of vortex, fresh electrolyte can easily transfer to microvias. Meanwhile, the ultrasonic treatment induced flow can decrease the thickness of the diffusion layer, and further improve the mass transfer efficiency of diffusion. Finally, with the enhancement of mass transfer, the quality of microvias electroforming is improved.

In order to quantitatively analyze the effect of ultrasonic agitation on the mass transfer, the mean velocity, which represents the space average velocity inside microvias, has been calculated. Fig. 3 shows statistical results of the mean velocity inside the microvias. The mean velocities are 4.11, 6.91, 9.31, 11.47 (10−4 m/s) with the ultrasonic powers of 100 W, 200 W, 300 W and 400 W, respectively. The variation tendency of mean velocity indicates that ultrasonic power enhances the mass transport in electroforming process. And the mass transfer increases with increasing ultrasonic power. Fig. 4 shows the pressure field in microvias under different ultrasonic powers (a) 100 W, (b) 200 W, (c) 300 W, (d) 400 W. The results show that the pressure field increases with increasing the ultrasonic power. The increased acoustic pressure increases the volume force, and further enhances the liquid flow. Therefore, the mass transfer is enhanced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of distance between the microvias and transducer
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of distance between the microvias and transducer on flow field has been investigated. The acoustic power of 200 W is utilized. Fig. 5 shows the mean liquid velocity for distance between the microvias and transducer of (a) 0.016 m, (b) 0.031 m, (c) 0.046 m, (d) 0.061 m, (e) 0.076 m. The mean velocities are 8.87, 7.49, 6.91, 6.32, 6.12 (10−4 m/s), respectively. Thus, the mean velocity decreases with the increase in distance between the microvias and transducer, which means that the enhancement effect of mass transfer decreases with the increase in distance between the microvias and transducer. It is considered that the pressure field inside the microvias will be weakened by the increasing of distance between the microvias and transducer, because of the absorption of ultrasonic waves in liquid. Additionally, the photoresist and metal substrate will also cause the attenuation of ultrasonic. In general, with the increasing of distance between the microvias and transducer, the acoustic pressure decreases, the volume force decreases, the liquid velocity decreases, which causes the enhancement effect of mass transfer decreasing. However, because the feature size of microvias is small, even if the minimum mean liquid velocity is 6.12 (10−4 m/s), the flow speed is sufficient to traverse inside the microvias. So, when the microvias electroforming is performed with ultrasonic agitation, the ultrasonic induced flow can provide all microvias with efficient mass transfer and improve the quality of microvias electroforming.

In order to investigate the influence of the photoresist structure on the ultrasonic treatment induced flow inside microvias, the microvias with different high aspect ratios were set in the simulation. The ultrasonic power of 200 W was utilized. Fig. 6 (a)–(c) shows the simulation results of liquid velocity distribution inside microvias for aspect ratios of (a) 1:2, (b) 3:2, (c) 4:1. The given microvias are located in the middle of the substrate. The mean velocities are 10.80, 6.91, 1.97 (10−4 m/s), respectively. The results indicate that the mass transfer of microvias electroforming decreases with the increasing of aspect ratio. This is because ultrasonic treatment induced flow gradually forms two vortexes when the depth of microvias is larger than 120 μm, and the vortex in the bottom of microvias is hard to be affected by the external flow and makes bubbles difficult to escape. Meanwhile, the attenuation function of ultrasonic treatment increases with increasing the aspect ratios. Fig. 7 shows the pressure field in microvias with aspect ratios at (a) 1:2, (b) 3:2 and (c) 4:1. The results show that the pressure field decreases with increasing the aspect ratio. Therefore, the ultrasonic induced flow becomes slower, and the enhancement effect of mass transfer decreases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the distance between cathode and central position
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the simulation results of mean velocities with different distances between cathode and central position. The mean velocities of microvias in the middle of the substrate are 6.91, 2.78, 1.70 (10−4 m/s), respectively. This means that the mass transfer of microvias electroforming decreases with the increasing of distance between cathode and central position. According to the simulation results of acoustic pressure distribution, the acoustic pressure decreases with the increasing distance and further, the enhancement on the ultrasonic induced flow becomes slow, which causes the decrease in ultrasonic enhancement on mass transfer.

In order to explore the electrode kinetics of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation and verify the simulation results, the electrochemical experiments are performed. The simulation results show that when the depth of microvias is larger than 120 μm, ultrasonic agitation induced flow gradually forms two vortexes, which decreases the mass transfer and makes bubbles difficult to escape. This also has a negative impact on the electrochemical measurements, therefore the microvias with a depth of 120 μm are used in the experiment. And because the transducers of commercial ultrasonic cleaning instrument are evenly distributed at the bottom of instrument, the distribution of the ultrasonic field is uniform at the same ultrasonic power. Hence, the electrochemical experiments are investigated without ultrasonic and with different ultrasonic powers.

During the electrochemical experiments, the working electrode was fabricated by UV-LIGA technology. The fabrication details of the working electrode are as follows: Firstly, photoresist layer was obtained by spinning SU-8 photoresist on the copper substrate at 1000 rpm for 30 s. The thickness is about 120 μm. Secondly, the photoresist layer was pre-baked at 85 °C for 90 min. After that, the photoresist was exposed to the UV light at 7.8 mW cm−2 for 50 s with photomask and then was post-baked at 85 °C for 3 min. Finally, the photoresist layer was developed in SU-8 developer. After development, the working electrode was obtained. The working electrode consisted of copper substrate, SU-8 photoresist and microvias array, as shown in Fig. 9 . The diameter and depth of microvias are 80 μm and 120 μm, respectively. The area of working electrode is 2.5 cm2.

Fig. 10 shows the schematic diagram of the experimental set-up, which consists of a commercial ultrasonic cleaning instrument (frequence, 120 kHz; power, 0–400 W), an electrolytic cell, a CHI 650E electrochemical workstation (CH Instrument Co. of Shanghai, China), computer and a conventional three-electrode system. The counter electrode was copper substrates with a thickness of 2 mm and an area of 36 cm2. In the electrolytic cell, the working and counter electrodes were positioned in parallel with a gap distance of 4 cm. And saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as reference electrode. In the experiments, the bath composition was copper sulfate 6.3 mmol/L, sulfuric acid 0.1 mol/L. The solutions were prepared by dissolving reagent grade chemicals in triple-distilled water. The bath temperature was maintained at 23 °C.

The cathodic polarization was carried out at a sweep rate of 0.003 V/s. The scan range of electric potential was 0.1 to −0.8 V. The electrochemical measurements were divided into two groups, with and without ultrasonic agitation respectively. Ultrasonic power was set as 100 W, 200 W, 300 W, 400 W.

The cathodic polarization curves with and without ultrasonic agitation are shown in Fig. 11 . The powers of the ultrasonic agitation are 100 W, 200 W, 300 W and 400 W, respectively. It is found that the changing trends of these curves are similar. As the potential becomes negative, the cathodic current rapidly increases until the concentration of copper ions on the electrode surface tends to zero. At this point, the current reaches a peak near −0.05 V. And then the current starts to decrease because of the meager effect [28] of copper ions. When the potential is swept to −0.2 V, a current plateau appears. Finally, when the potential is smaller than −0.7 V, the cathodic current density increases rapidly and the hydrogen reaction begins to occur. In this paper, the limiting diffusion current density was observed at potentials between −0.2 V and −0.6 V. From the polarization curves it is known that the limiting diffusion current density without ultrasonic agitation is 4.23 A/m2. The limiting diffusion current densities are 8.46 A/m2, 11.78 A/m2, 13.79 A/m2 and 16.61 A/m2 with the ultrasonic powers of 100 W, 200 W, 300 W and 400 W, respectively. It is noted that ultrasonic agitation can significantly increase the limiting diffusion current density. It is well known that the current plateau in the cathode polarization curve is controlled by the mass transfer [29]. Thus, this fact indicates that ultrasonic agitation can increase the limiting diffusion current density and further enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming.

From the cathodic polarization curves, the limiting diffusion current density has been quantitatively studied. On the other hand, promotion of mass transport is regarded as being equivalent to decrease in diffusion layer thickness [20]. Thus, it is attempted below to estimate a difference in the thickness between without ultrasonic and under different-powers ultrasonic agitation. It is well known that the equation of limiting diffusion current density can be described as [30]:
(11)
i =
nFCD
δ
where
δ
is diffusion layer thickness,
i
represents limiting diffusion current density,
n
,
F
,
C
and
D
denote electrochemical valence, Faraday constant, concentration, diffusion coefficient. Since
n
,
F
,
C
and
D
are constant under experimental conditions given, the diffusion layer thickness under silence and different ultrasonic powers can be obtained. The diffusion coefficient is equal to 0.7 × 10−9 m2/s. The calculation results of diffusion layer thickness are presented in Table 2 . Compared with the silent condition, the diffusion layer thicknesses with the acoustic power of 100 W, 200 W, 300 W, 400 W are decreased by 50.0%, 64.1%, 69.3% and 74.5%, respectively. It is also indicated that ultrasonic agitation can enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming, and the enhancement of mass transfer increases with acoustic power. And the experimental results are consistent with the simulation results, and prove the correctness of the simulation indirectly.

Ultrasonic wave is a kind of longitudinal wave, which can produce acoustic pressure with periodic positive and negative alternately. The acoustic pressure can cause the periodic vibration of the electroforming solution. When the vibrating electroforming solution transmits to the surface of the cathode and the sidewall of the photoresist, the amplitude is rapidly changed, and causes a steady flow [31]. Meanwhile, cavitation nuclei can be nucleated by trapping gas in microvias, which makes cavitation bubbles easily form inside the microvias [32,33]. Under the effect of acoustic pressure, the cavitation bubbles expand and contract until collapse, forming a liquid jet or a pressure wave [34]. Through the two ways above, ultrasonic agitation decreases the diffusion layer thickness, increases the limiting diffusion current density, and further enhances the mass transfer of microvias electroforming.

And the research has shown that the ultrasonic power has a direct effect on the intensity of acoustic streaming and ultrasonic cavitation [31]. With the increase in ultrasonic power, the enhanced acoustic pressure provides the solution with the greater volume force and causes the increase in acoustic streaming. Meanwhile, the diameters of cavitation bubbles become larger, which leads to the increase in ultrasonic cavitation intensity. Besides, equivalent flow velocity has been proposed to describe the convective mass transfer induced by ultrasonic agitation (ultrasonic wind and cavitation activity), which is proportional to ultrasonic power and the square of limiting diffusion current [35,36]. This means that the higher the ultrasonic power is, the larger the equivalent flow velocity and the faster the convective mass transfer will be. In conclusion, the increase in the ultrasonic power further decreases the diffusion layer thickness, increases the limiting diffusion current density and strengthens the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. And, due to the enhanced mass transfer, the problems of porous structure and electroforming failure are solved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the metal micro-column array structures
ce:section.ce:para: Metal micro-column array structures have good potential applications in micro electrical discharge machining [2], clinical investigation [3] and compression molding for multiple micro-holes [6], etc. Consequently, the processing technology of the metal micro-column array structures has received more and more attention. UV-LIGA is an effective way to fabricate metal micro-column array structures [37,38]. In UV-LIGA process, the micro-column array structures are usually fabricated by electroforming in the microvias array photoresist structures. However, the poor mass transfer of microvias electroforming seriously affects the product yield and usability of metal micro-column array structures. Hence, in order to investigate the feasibility of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation, the 200 × 200 metal micro-column array structures are fabricated by ultrasonic electroforming. The metal micro-column has a diameter of 80 μm and height of 250 μm.

Considering it takes a long time to finish the electroformed metal micro-column array structures with 250 μm high, the high temperature and pressure caused by ultrasonic agitation may destroy the photoresist mold, causing the fabrication failure of the structure. Hence, in this paper, the ultrasonic power of 200 W is chosen to fabricate metal micro-column array structures. And in order to compare the improvement effect, a metal micro-column array structures is fabricated by electroforming without ultrasonic. Methods such as agitation by moving cathode, pulse-reversed current have been used in the electroforming.

The fabrication process includes fabrication of microvias array photoresist structures and microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation. For fabrication details of microvias array photoresist structures refer to Section 3.1. And then micro-column array structures were fabricated by ultrasonic electroforming in the microvias array photoresist structures. Through three time fabrication of microvias array photoresist structures and three time electroforming process, the metal micro-column array structures with 250 μm high were finished. At last, the photoresist were removed and metal micro-column array structures could be obtained.

Fig. 12 (a) and (b) shows the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of metal micro-column array structures without ultrasonic and with ultrasonic waves. Compared with the micro-column array structures with ultrasonic, it is found that the micro-column array structures without ultrasonic have poor electroforming layer. And there remain the problems of porous structure and electroforming failure. Because when the electroforming is performed in the microvias, the poor mass transfer makes microvias easy to form porous structure or even lead to the failure of electroforming. In contrast, when the ultrasonic is introduced to the microvias electroforming, the mass transfer is enhanced, and then the problems of porous structure and electroforming failure are solved effectively. The results of fabrication confirm that the ultrasonic treatment can enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming, and the results are consistent with the previous simulation and experimental results.

Microvias electroforming with ultrasonic agitation is investigated by simulations and electrochemical experiments. And the micro-column array structures are fabricated to prove the feasibility of microvias electroforming under ultrasonic agitation. The conclusions are as follows:
(1)
The liquid velocity distribution of ultrasonic induced flow inside the microvias is numerically simulated. Simulation results indicate that the mean liquid velocity inside the microvias increases with the increasing of acoustic power. In addition, under a certain ultrasonic power, the mean liquid velocity will decrease with increasing the distance between microvias and transducer, as the same with increasing the aspect ratio of microvias, and the distance between cathode and central position.
(2)
Electrochemical experiments indicate that ultrasonic agitation can decrease the diffusion layer thickness, increase the limiting diffusion current densities and further enhance the mass transfer of microvias electroforming. Compared with the silent condition, the diffusion layer thicknesses with the acoustic power of 100 W, 200 W, 300 W, 400 W are decreased by 50.0%, 64.1%, 69.3% and 74.5%, respectively.
(3)
Based on the simulation and experimental results, the 200 × 200 metal micro-column array structures are fabricated by ultrasonic electroforming in the microvias array photoresist structures. The diameter and height are 80 μm and 250 μm respectively. As a result, ultrasonic enhances the mass transfer and improves the quality of electroforming layer, which is consistent with the simulation and experimental results.
(4)
The electrochemical experiments mainly investigated the effect of ultrasonic powers on the electrode kinetics of microvias electroforming because of the limit of commercial ultrasonic cleaning instrument. More electrochemical experiments of effect parameters will be studied in the future work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NOs. 51375077, 51475245) and Science Fund for Creative Research Groups of NSFC (NO. 51621064).
